description: Greenstar Si Compact, Greenstar i, Greenstar 4000 and CDi Classic ranges. Codes cover combi, system and regular models.

faultCodes.title: Pump running dry or seized
faultCodes.summary: Pump's running dry or not moving water — either seized, airlocked, or system's empty.
likelyCauses: System pressure too low or empty
Pump shaft seized (magnetic rotor stuck after long idle period)
Auto air vent blocked or capped
quickChecks: Check system pressure — should be 1.0–1.5 bar cold
Listen for pump running when boiler fires
Bleed radiators and auto air vent on top of pump
Free a seized pump by loosening the centre screw and rotating the shaft with a flat screwdriver

faultCodes.title: Hot water NTC sensor defective
faultCodes.summary: Hot water sensor is reading wrong — open or short circuit.
likelyCauses: NTC sensor open or short circuit
quickChecks: Unplug and re-seat the DHW NTC connector
Meter the NTC — should be 8k–12kΩ at room temp
Inspect the loom for damage near the heat exchanger

faultCodes.title: Lost connection to room controls
faultCodes.summary: Boiler's lost connection to the room controller, FX programmer or weather comp controller.
likelyCauses: Broken or shorted data cable to the controller
quickChecks: Check continuity of the two data wires between boiler and controller
Power-cycle the boiler and controller
Swap in a known-good controller if one is available

faultCodes.title: Code plug missing or not detected
faultCodes.summary: Boiler can't read the coding plug. Won't fire without it.
likelyCauses: Coding plug removed during a service
Damaged or wrong coding plug after a PCB swap
quickChecks: Turn boiler off at the spur, re-seat the coding plug firmly, and power back on
Confirm the coding plug matches the model rating plate
Check for broken pins on the plug

faultCodes.title: Fan speed too low
faultCodes.summary: Fan isn't hitting the speed the boiler wants. Could be bearings, flue, or wiring.
likelyCauses: Blocked flue or heavy condensate in the flue
quickChecks: Listen and feel the fan on start-up — rough bearings are obvious
Inspect flue terminal for blockage (nests, debris)
Drain condensate from the flue low point if fitted
Check 230V supply to the fan and the tacho signal back to the PCB

faultCodes.title: Return sensor reading wrong
faultCodes.summary: Return NTC is giving a dodgy reading.
quickChecks: Re-seat the return NTC plug
Measure resistance against the NTC table
Check for water ingress at the sensor

faultCodes.title: Central heating flow NTC defective
faultCodes.summary: Flow NTC is open, shorted, or reading wrong.
likelyCauses: Flow NTC open or short circuit
quickChecks: Unplug, inspect and re-seat the flow NTC
Measure resistance at room temperature (~10kΩ typical)
Compare reading on the service menu with actual pipe temperature

faultCodes.title: Overheat — flow temp too high
faultCodes.summary: Flow temp's gone too high — overheat stat or sensor has tripped. Almost always a circulation problem.
likelyCauses: Poor circulation (seized pump, airlock, closed valves)
Limescale or sludge in the primary heat exchanger
quickChecks: Confirm pump is circulating and system is vented
Check inhibitor level and inspect for sludge (black water on expansion vessel drain)
Feel flow and return pipes — large temperature differential points to poor flow
Swap the overheat stat if it reads open cold

faultCodes.title: No flame on ignition
faultCodes.summary: Boiler sparked but couldn't see a flame. Most common lockout — usually gas, electrode, or condensate.
likelyCauses: Gas off — check ECV and meter
Condensate trap or pipework blocked, drowning the burner
Electrode dirty, cracked, or wrong gap
Ignition lead cracked or shorting out
Wrong polarity at the spur (live and neutral swapped)
quickChecks: Check gas is on and other gas appliances run normally
Check polarity at the spur
Clean the flame sensor and check spark gap (3–4mm)
Clean out the condensate trap — make sure it's draining
Check gas pressure at the test point

faultCodes.title: False flame when boiler's off
faultCodes.summary: Boiler thinks there's a flame when the gas is off. Usually a damp lead or cracked electrode.
quickChecks: Dry and inspect the ignition lead and electrode
Check electrode for cracks in the porcelain
Watch the burner with gas off — if you see gas, the valve's leaking through

faultCodes.title: PCB internal fault
faultCodes.summary: PCB's had an internal fault. Usually moisture or hardware failure.
quickChecks: Turn the boiler off for 60 seconds and back on
Look at the PCB — water marks, green corrosion, or burnt bits?
Check mains voltage

faultCodes.title: Flame detected while boiler is off
faultCodes.summary: Same as EB — boiler's seeing a flame when it shouldn't. Damp lead or dodgy electrode.
likelyCauses: Gas valve passing gas when closed
quickChecks: Dry out the burner chamber and lead
Inspect electrode for cracks
Soap-test the burner when the gas valve is closed

faultCodes.title: Flame detected after gas shut off
faultCodes.summary: Gas valve closed but boiler still sees flame — valve might be leaking through.
quickChecks: Soap test the burner with the boiler off to confirm valve closure
Dry and inspect the ignition lead
Replace the gas valve if it is proven to be passing

faultCodes.title: Reset button pressed in error
faultCodes.summary: The boiler logs a reset that was pressed without a prior lockout.
quickChecks: No action needed — clears automatically

faultCodes.title: Low system pressure warning
faultCodes.summary: Pressure has dropped close to the low cut-off but the boiler is still running.
quickChecks: Top up the system to 1.2 bar cold
Walk radiators and visible pipework for weeping joints
Check the PRV outside discharge pipe for dripping

faultCodes.title: Waiting for fan to prove airflow
faultCodes.summary: Fan hasn't proved airflow yet. Normal if it's brief — if it hangs, check fan and flue.
quickChecks: Confirm fan is actually spinning on start-up
Inspect the flue run for debris
Re-seat the fan connector and tacho lead

faultCodes.title: Ignition lockout
faultCodes.summary: The boiler has failed to ignite within the allowed number of attempts. This is the most common Worcester fault code — usually gas supply, electrode, or condensate related.
likelyCauses: No gas supply — check meter, ECV, and other gas appliances
Ignition electrodes worn, cracked, or dirty
Flame sense electrode dirty or glazed — won't detect flame
Condensate trap or pipe blocked — backs up and floods the burner
Gas valve not opening — faulty coil or sticking valve
Frozen condensate pipe — extremely common in winter
quickChecks: Confirm gas at other appliances (hob, fire) — if no gas anywhere, contact supplier
Clean ignition and flame-sense electrodes with fine emery cloth
Check condensate trap and external condensate pipe — thaw if frozen
Measure gas pressure at burner test point under load — should be 20 mbar for natural gas
Reset once and observe — if it sparks but doesn't light, suspect gas supply; if lights then goes out, suspect flame sense electrode

faultCodes.title: Flow temperature too high
faultCodes.summary: Flow temp's gone above the limit. Usually a circulation problem — pump, airlock, or sludge.
quickChecks: Check pump operation and system pressure
Vent rads and auto air vent
Inspect heat exchanger for sludge evidence

faultCodes.title: Fan running too fast
faultCodes.summary: Fan's spinning too fast. Tacho signal or fan fault.
likelyCauses: High winds at the flue (comes and goes)
quickChecks: Check fan tacho wiring and connector
Run the boiler in service test to confirm commanded vs actual speed

faultCodes.title: Fan running too slow
faultCodes.summary: Fan's not hitting the right speed.
quickChecks: Spin the fan by hand when isolated — grinding or stiff = bearings
Check supply voltage to the fan
Clean the fan impeller if accessible

faultCodes.title: Fan start-up problem
faultCodes.summary: Fan didn't start when the boiler called for heat.
quickChecks: Confirm 230V supply at the fan connector on call for heat
Spin the fan manually and listen for bearing noise
Re-seat all fan connections

faultCodes.title: Flow/return temp gap too big
faultCodes.summary: Big gap between flow and return temps — water's not circulating properly.
likelyCauses: Heat exchanger blocked with limescale or sludge
quickChecks: Confirm pump is running at normal speed
Check all isolation valves are fully open
Inspect heat exchanger flow passages if accessible

faultCodes.title: Flow temp rising too fast
faultCodes.summary: Flow temp's rising too fast — not enough water moving past the sensor.
quickChecks: Bleed the auto air vent
Spin the pump shaft by hand if cold
Verify all isolation valves are fully open

faultCodes.title: Mains power error / electrical interruption
faultCodes.summary: Power cut or spike knocked the boiler out.
likelyCauses: Unstable mains supply or power cut
Loose wiring at spur or plug
Faulty PCB unable to regulate power
quickChecks: Check mains voltage at the boiler spur — should be 230V +/-
Inspect wiring at fused spur for loose connections
If power stable, reset — if it recurs, suspect PCB

faultCodes.title: Temp gap too big
faultCodes.summary: Flow and return temps are too far apart — circulation problem.
likelyCauses: Pump not running at correct speed or failing
quickChecks: Check pump is running and speed is adequate
Feel the flow and return pipes — large temp difference confirms circulation issue
Check for system blockage — flush if needed

faultCodes.title: Fan stopped mid-run
faultCodes.summary: Fan stopped while the boiler was running.
quickChecks: Listen for the fan — is it spinning at all when the boiler fires?
Check the fan connector is fully plugged in
Measure voltage at fan leads — should see ~240V AC when demanded

faultCodes.title: Gas valve wiring fault
faultCodes.summary: Gas valve wiring disconnected or broken.
likelyCauses: Loose connector on gas valve coil
Broken wire in gas valve harness
quickChecks: Check gas valve connector is fully seated
Measure gas valve coil resistance — typically 30-60Ω per coil
Inspect harness for damaged or broken wires

faultCodes.title: No pressure change from pump
faultCodes.summary: Pump started but pressure didn't change — pump's stuck or sensor's faulty.
likelyCauses: Very low system pressure (below sensor range)
quickChecks: Check system pressure on the gauge — repressurise if below 1 bar
Listen for the pump — if silent, it may be seized
Tap the pump gently to free it if stuck

faultCodes.title: Flue temp too high
faultCodes.summary: Flue gas temp's gone too high. Could be blocked heat exchanger or wrong gas rate.
likelyCauses: Heat exchanger blocked up with scale or sludge
quickChecks: Check the flue for obstructions or blockages
Do a combustion test — check CO/CO₂
Inspect the heat exchanger for signs of scaling or blockage

faultCodes.title: Flue temp rising too fast
faultCodes.summary: Flue temp's climbing too fast. Check heat exchanger and gas pressure.
likelyCauses: Heat exchanger blocked with sludge or scale
quickChecks: Check gas pressure at the burner — compare to data badge spec
Inspect heat exchanger for signs of blockage
Check flue gas sensor resistance against spec

faultCodes.title: Flow temp over 110°C
faultCodes.summary: Flow temp hit 110°C — emergency shutdown. Check circulation and pump.
likelyCauses: No circulation — pump's dead or system's full of sludge
quickChecks: Check pump is running — is water circulating?
Check for system blockages — feel pipes for cold spots
If pump OK and water flowing, suspect sensor or heat exchanger

faultCodes.title: Pump communication lost
faultCodes.summary: Boiler can't talk to the pump. Check the wiring between PCB and pump.
likelyCauses: Loose wiring between PCB and pump
Wrong pump fitted — not compatible with this model
quickChecks: Check wires to pump are secure — reconnect and re-power
Verify the correct pump type is fitted for the model
Reset the boiler and monitor

faultCodes.title: DHW sensor fault
faultCodes.summary: Hot water sensor reading wrong.
likelyCauses: Wiring damage between sensor and PCB
quickChecks: Check DHW sensor wiring and connector for damage
Measure sensor resistance — should be ~10kΩ at 25°C
If resistance is wrong, replace the sensor

faultCodes.title: Outside sensor fault
faultCodes.summary: Outside sensor disconnected or reading wrong.
likelyCauses: Outdoor sensor disconnected or cable damaged
Sensor exposed to direct sunlight giving false readings
quickChecks: Check the outdoor sensor wiring and connections at the boiler
Verify sensor is positioned on a north-facing wall, out of direct sunlight
Measure sensor resistance — compare to manufacturer's chart

faultCodes.title: Flame sensor shorted
faultCodes.summary: Flame sensor's shorting to earth. Check for contact with metalwork or cracks in the insulator.
likelyCauses: Lead touching metalwork or the burner
Soot or carbon bridging the electrode
quickChecks: Check the electrode and lead — any cracks or touching metal?
Clean the electrode tip and check for carbon deposits
Meter electrode to earth — should be open circuit cold

flowcharts.title: Hot water flow sensor — voltage test
safetyWarnings: 230V present on adjacent terminals — take care with probes
Leave cover off only as long as needed, refit securely afterwards

steps.body: Turn the gas off. Take the front cover off and drop the control box cover. Find the flow sensor plug on the cold inlet — three wires (red, black, yellow).
Set your meter to DC volts (20V range). Back-probe the plug while it's still in the PCB.
Check the supply. Red lead on red wire, black on black. This is the 5V feed from the PCB to the sensor.
Now check the signal wire. Move red to the yellow wire, keep black on black. With no water flowing it should sit near one rail.
steps.expected: Close to 5V or close to 0V at rest

steps.body: Open a hot tap fully. You should hear the turbine spinning in the flow sensor. The meter should start reading mid-range.
With the tap running, read yellow-to-black voltage.
steps.expected: Averages around 2.5V DC while water is flowing

steps.body: No 5V from the PCB. Check the plug for water and the wiring back to the board. If the cable's fine, the PCB flow sensor circuit's gone.
Sensor's got power but isn't pulsing. Flow sensor's dead — replace it.
Supply and signal look good. Sensor's working. If hot water still isn't coming through, check the diverter valve, plate heat exchanger, or DHW NTC next.

flowcharts.title: No ignition lockout (EA / ignition fault)
safetyWarnings: Prove gas isolation before opening combustion chamber

steps.body: Are other gas appliances in the property working normally?

options.label: No, no gas at other appliances

steps.body: Reset the boiler and watch it. You should hear the fan purge, then sparks, then it either lights or locks out.
Are you hearing ignition sparks?
Does the burner light at all before locking out?
Lights then drops out = flame's not being sensed. Check the flame sensor for soot, cracks, or wrong gap. Check the lead and earth path.
Sparks but never lights = no gas getting to the burner, or gas valve not opening. Put a manometer on the test point and call for heat.
Check inlet gas pressure at the test point with the boiler calling for heat.
steps.expected: Approximately 20 mbar natural gas, 37 mbar LPG (within manufacturer tolerance)

steps.body: Gas supply problem before the boiler. Check ECV, meter, and any shut-off valves. Low pressure? Check pipe sizing or look for a partially closed valve.
No sparks. Check the ignition lead, electrode gap, and PCB ignition output. Cracked electrode or knackered lead is the usual culprit.
Flame's there but boiler can't see it. Clean or swap the flame sensor, check earth path to the PCB, and check ionisation current in service mode.
Gas is arriving but not getting to the burner. Check gas valve coil resistance and that it's getting 230V when the boiler calls for heat. Swap the gas valve if it's faulty.

flowcharts.title: Low system pressure
safetyWarnings: Isolate power before working on the expansion vessel
Hot water in the system — let it cool before draining

steps.body: Top up to 1.2 bar cold. Ask the customer when they last topped up.
Has the pressure been dropping over weeks, or quickly (within days)?
Gradual drops usually mean the expansion vessel. Turn the power off. Drain the system down to 0 bar.
With system drained, check the expansion vessel charge at the Schrader valve.
Walk every rad valve, pipe joint, and check the boiler internals for leaks. Check the PRV discharge pipe outside.
Found a visible leak?

options.label: Yes, found a wet joint or valve

steps.body: Expansion vessel's lost its charge. Re-pump it to spec. If water comes out the Schrader valve, the diaphragm's gone — replace the vessel.
Fix the leak, refill, vent, and re-pressurise to 1.2 bar. Check again in 24 hours.
No visible leak and vessel's fine. Probably a buried leak under floors. Pressure test with the boiler isolated, or try trace dye.
PRV's letting water out. Most likely the expansion vessel's gone — check that first. If vessel's fine, PRV seat might have debris, or someone left the filling loop open.

flowcharts.title: No central heating, hot water works

steps.body: Does the boiler fire on a hot tap but stay silent on a heating demand?
When heating calls, does the pump run?

options.label: Pump runs, boiler does not fire

steps.body: Diverter might be stuck on hot water. Cycle the heating a few times and feel the valve — you should hear or feel the actuator move.
Does the diverter actuator move when heating calls?
Boiler's not firing for anything. Different fault — use the no-ignition flowchart.
No demand reaching the boiler. Check stat wiring, programmer, and that heating's selected. Try bridging the CH demand terminals.
Diverter actuator's not moving on CH demand. Check 230V to the actuator, then the motor. Replace actuator or cartridge if needed.
Actuator moves but boiler won't fire on CH. Check flow NTC and overheat stat in service mode. If flow sensor reads high when the pipe's cold, replace it.

flowcharts.title: Fan fault (C6 / 265 / 266 / 283)
safetyWarnings: Isolate mains before unplugging fan connections

steps.body: Take the case off and power the boiler. Call for heat and watch the fan.
Does the fan spin up at all?
Fan spins fine but still locking out. Points to the tacho signal, not the motor.
Turn the power off and unplug the fan. Get your meter on the winding wires.
Check resistance across the fan winding pins.
steps.expected: Typically 10–80 Ω depending on model (must match the service manual figure)

steps.body: Bearings worn — fan assembly needs replacing.
Fan spins but PCB can't see the tacho. Check the tacho wire. If wiring's good, replace the fan assembly (tacho's built in).
Winding's open — motor's dead. Replace the fan.
Fan winding's fine but it won't spin. Check for 230V at the fan plug when the boiler calls for heat. No voltage? Check the loom and PCB fan driver.

flowcharts.title: Frozen condensate pipe
toolsNeeded: Hot water jug or hot water bottle
safetyWarnings: Do not use boiling water on plastic condensate pipe — warm tap water only

steps.body: External condensate pipe outside? Cold weather and boiler locked out with EA or similar?

options.label: Yes, pipe outside in a frost event

steps.body: Find the outside section. Feel for where the ice plug is — usually at the bottom of a vertical run, at a bend, or where the pipe ends.
Pour warm (not boiling) water over the ice plug. Start from the top and work down. Listen for water starting to flow.
Reset the boiler. It should now run normally.
Tell the customer: get the condensate rerouted inside, upsize to 32mm pipe outside, insulate it properly, or fit trace heating. Quote the job.
Condensate is run inside, so it won't be frozen. Check the internal trap instead — clean it out.
Pipe thawed, boiler running. Recommend an upgrade so it doesn't happen again.

flowcharts.title: PRV leaking / excess pressure
safetyWarnings: Isolate the boiler flow and return before draining down. Do not attempt expansion vessel recharge with the system pressurised.
Always follow the manufacturer's instructions for the correct expansion vessel pre-charge pressure — typically 0.5–1.0 bar.

steps.body: Shut the boiler flow and return valves. Open the drain-off to get the system down to zero.
With system at zero, put a tyre gauge on the expansion vessel Schrader valve. What's the charge?
Pump the vessel up to the maker's spec. Leave it 5 mins to check it holds. Spray leak detector on the Schrader valve.
Does the expansion vessel hold the recharged pressure for 5 minutes without dropping?
Re-pressurise, open the valves, fire on heating and let it get up to temp. Watch the pressure gauge.
Does the pressure still rise above 3 bar or is the PRV still discharging while the boiler heats up?

options.label: No — pressure stays in normal range

steps.body: Disconnect the filling loop or check that it is fully closed. With the filling loop disconnected, does the pressure still rise while the boiler is cold/idle?

options.label: Yes — still rising with filling loop off
No — pressure stable after disconnecting filling loop

steps.body: Vessel diaphragm's gone — can't hold charge. Replace the vessel. Swap the PRV too while you're there.
Vessel recharge sorted it. The PRV was dumping because the flat vessel couldn't take the expansion. Keep an eye on it. Swap the PRV as good practice.
Plate heat exchanger's leaking inside — mains pressure getting into the heating circuit. Replace the plate. Swap the PRV too.
Filling loop was letting mains in. Replace it or close it properly. On some models, check for an integral filling link.

flowcharts.title: No hot water — heating works fine
safetyWarnings: Isolate the electrical supply before testing any wiring. Take care around hot pipework.

steps.body: Turn on a hot tap and observe the boiler. Does the boiler attempt to fire for hot water (fan starts, ignition attempts)?

options.label: Yes — boiler fires but water stays cold
No — boiler doesn't respond to hot tap demand

steps.body: Boiler's firing but hot water's not coming through. Feel the pipes — is the flow to the outlet warm but the tap's cold?

options.label: Flow pipe warm, tap cold — diverter's not switching
Everything heating up but water's still lukewarm

steps.body: Water's lukewarm — could be a blocked plate heat exchanger or flow rate issue. Been descaled recently? Is mains pressure OK?

options.label: Poor mains flow — low pressure at taps
Mains flow fine — suspect blocked plate

steps.body: The boiler doesn't recognise a hot water demand. Does the boiler show any fault codes on the display?

options.label: No fault code — boiler just ignores the hot tap

steps.body: Check the flow sensor. Open a hot tap and listen — can you hear the turbine spinning? On Greenstars, look for 1–6V at the sensor plug.

options.label: No signal / no spin — flow sensor's dead

steps.body: Diverter's not switching to hot water mode. Check for 240V at the diverter motor when hot water's demanded. Got power but not moving? Replace the motor or cartridge.
Mains water pressure's too low. Boiler needs enough flow to trigger DHW mode. Check the stopcock's fully open. Might need a boost pump or a plumber.
Plate heat exchanger's blocked with limescale — common in hard water areas. Sometimes you can flush it with descaler, but often it needs replacing.
Fault code showing — look it up in the Fault Finder for that brand.
Flow sensor's not picking up water flow. Replace it. On Greenstars, check the plug for corrosion too.
Flow sensor's sending a signal but the PCB's ignoring it. Try a reset first. If it keeps doing it, PCB might need replacing — call the manufacturer.

flowcharts.title: Boiler kettling or banging noises
toolsNeeded: Stethoscope or ear against pipe (careful — hot!)
safetyWarnings: Pipework may be very hot — use caution when touching or listening. Never put your ear directly on hot pipes.

steps.body: When does the noise occur? Fire the boiler on heating and listen carefully.

options.label: Bang or pop on ignition only
Banging from the pipes, not the boiler

steps.body: Kettling usually means limescale in the heat exchanger or poor circulation. Has the system ever been flushed or had inhibitor?
Check pump speed and circulation. Pump on the right speed? All rad valves open? Good flow/return gap (10–20°C)?

options.label: Temp gap too big (>25°C) — poor flow

steps.body: Banging on ignition = delayed ignition. Gas builds up before the spark catches. Check electrode gap, clean the burner, check gas pressure. Clogged burner? Clean or replace it.
Banging from pipes = thermal expansion. Pipes pushing against joists or boards. Felt or pipe clips where it's rubbing. Not a boiler fault.
Limescale and sludge in the heat exchanger — classic kettling. Powerflush the system and dose with inhibitor. If it's really bad, heat exchanger might need replacing.
Poor flow's causing the heat exchanger to overheat locally. Check pump speed, all valves open, no blockages. Might need a flush. Fit a magnetic filter.
Flow's fine but still kettling — heat exchanger's scaled up inside and a flush didn't shift it. Common in hard water areas. Might need a new heat exchanger or a chemical descale.
